title: Flow law sensitivity analysis
code: edot_estimates0 <- sapply(   models,   function(m) {     creep_quartz(       stress = stress0,        temperature = temperature,        fugacity = fugacity,        pressure = pressure,        grainsize = grainsize,        model = m,        propagate_err = FALSE)   } ) |>   setNames(models) |>   t() |>   t() |>   as.data.frame() |>   rename(`50%` = V1)  edot_estimates0$model <- models edot_estimates0$error <- "none"

what: Only error propagation of flow law uncertainties
title: Flow law sensitivity analysis
text: Specify amount Monte Carlo (MC) simulations: Recalculate stress using MC simulations:
code: n <- 1e3 stress1 <- grainsize_piezometry(grainsize, sim = n)

previous_headings: Only error propagation of flow law uncertainties
title: Flow law sensitivity analysis
text: Visualize calculated stress estimates:  Calculate strain rates using stress estimates:
code: stress_log <- log10(as.numeric(stress1))  hist(stress_log, xlab = bquote(sigma[d] ~ "(MPa)"),       main = NULL, freq = FALSE, xaxt = "n", border = "white") ticks <- seq(1.4, 2.6, .2) labels <- sapply(ticks, function(i) as.expression(bquote(10^.(i)))) axis(1, at = ticks, labels = labels)  lines(density(stress_log), lwd = 1.5, col = "dodgerblue3", xpd = TRUE) abline(v = median(stress_log), lty = 2, lwd = 2, col = "red") edot_estimates1 <- lapply(   models,   function(m) {     creep_quartz(       stress = median(stress1),        temperature = temperature,        fugacity = fugacity,        pressure = pressure,        grainsize = grainsize,        model = m,        sim = n)   } ) |>   setNames(models)

previous_headings: Only error propagation of flow law uncertainties
title: Flow law sensitivity analysis
text: Visualize strain rate estimates:
code: m <- "Hirth2001" edot_log <- log10(as.numeric(edot_estimates1[[m]]))  hist(edot_log, xlab = bquote(dot(epsilon) ~ "(" * s^{   -1 } * ")"), main = m, freq = FALSE, xaxt = "n", border = "white")  ticks <- seq(-22, -6, 2) labels <- sapply(ticks, function(i) as.expression(bquote(10^.(i)))) axis(1, at = ticks, labels = labels)  lines(density(edot_log), lwd = 1.5, col = "#4E79A7", xpd = TRUE) abline(v = quantile(edot_log, probs = c(.025, .5, .975)), lty = 2, lwd = 2, col = "#E15759") edot_quantiles1 <- sapply(edot_estimates1, function(x) {   qs <- quantile(x, probs = c(.025, .5, .975))   m <- exp(mean(log(x)))   c(qs, mean = m) }) |>   t() |>   as.data.frame()  # edot_quantiles1$model <- reorder(factor(models), edot_quantiles1$`50%`)\ edot_quantiles1$model <- models edot_quantiles1$error <- "only flow law"

what: Error propagation of flow law uncertainties and measurement errors
title: Flow law sensitivity analysis
text: Now add uncertainties temperature pressure estimates, recalculate fugacity: (may take …)
code: temperature2 <- units::set_units(rnorm(n, temperature, 50), degC) pressure2 <- units::set_units(rnorm(n, pressure, 100), MPa) fugacity2 <- ps_fugacity(pressure2, temperature2, future.seed = TRUE)

previous_headings: Error propagation of flow law uncertainties and measurement errors
title: Flow law sensitivity analysis
text: Visualize distribution recalculated fugacity values:  Student’s T-test normal distribution:
code: fug_bar <- log10(as.numeric(fugacity2))  hist(fug_bar, xlab = bquote("f"["H"[2] * "O"] ~ "(bar)"),       main = "Pitzer and Sterner (1994)",       freq = FALSE, xaxt = "n",       border = "white")  ticks <- seq(1, 4, .5) labels <- sapply(ticks, function(i) as.expression(bquote(10^.(i)))) axis(1, at = ticks, labels = labels)  lines(density(fug_bar), lwd = 1.5, col = "#4E79A7", xpd = TRUE) abline(v = quantile(fug_bar, probs = c(.025, .5, .975)), lty = 2, lwd = 2, col = "#E15759") t.test(as.numeric(fugacity2)) #>  #>  One Sample t-test #>  #> data:  as.numeric(fugacity2) #> t = 49.629, df = 999, p-value < 2.2e-16 #> alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 0 #> 95 percent confidence interval: #>  414.6980 448.8424 #> sample estimates: #> mean of x  #>  431.7702 t.test(log10(as.numeric(fugacity2))) #>  #>  One Sample t-test #>  #> data:  log10(as.numeric(fugacity2)) #> t = 282.68, df = 999, p-value < 2.2e-16 #> alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 0 #> 95 percent confidence interval: #>  2.531073 2.566459 #> sample estimates: #> mean of x  #>  2.548766

previous_headings: Error propagation of flow law uncertainties and measurement errors
title: Flow law sensitivity analysis
text: Calculate strain rate values: Visualize distribution new strian rate values:
code: edot_estimates2 <- lapply(   models,   function(m) {     creep_quartz(       stress = stress1,        temperature = temperature2,        fugacity = fugacity2,        pressure = pressure2,        grainsize = grainsize,        model = m,        sim = n)   } ) |>   setNames(models) m <- "Hirth2001" edot_log2 <- log10(as.numeric(edot_estimates2[[m]]))  hist(edot_log2, xlab = bquote(dot(epsilon) ~ "(" * s^{   -1 } * ")"), main = m, freq = FALSE, xaxt = "n", border = "white")  ticks <- seq(-22, -6, 2) labels <- sapply(ticks, function(i) as.expression(bquote(10^.(i)))) axis(1, at = ticks, labels = labels)  lines(density(edot_log2), lwd = 1.5, col = "#4E79A7", xpd = TRUE) abline(v = quantile(edot_log2, probs = c(.025, .5, .975)), lty = 2, lwd = 2, col = "#E15759") edot_quantiles2 <- sapply(edot_estimates2, function(x) {   qs <- quantile(x, probs = c(.025, .5, .975))   m <- exp(mean(log(x)))   c(qs, mean = m) }) |>   t() |>   as.data.frame()  edot_quantiles2$model <- models edot_quantiles2$error <- "all"

previous_headings: Error propagation of flow law uncertainties and measurement errors
title: Flow law sensitivity analysis
text: Finally, compare strain rate results calculated without error propagation, considering errors flow laws, uncertainties initial temperature pressure values:
code: bind_rows(edot_quantiles1, edot_quantiles2, edot_estimates0) |>   mutate(     # model = reorder(factor(model), `50%`),     error = factor(error, levels = c("none", "only flow law", "all"))   ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = `50%`, xmin = `2.5%`, xmax = `97.5%`, y = model, color = error)) +   geom_rect(aes(xmin = 1e-15, xmax = 1e-14, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf), color = NA, fill = "grey90") +   annotate("text",             x = 10^(-14.5),             y = length(models) / 2,             label = "Natural geological strain rates",             angle = 90, hjust = .5, vjust = .5, color = "grey50", fontface = 3) +   geom_linerange(position = position_dodge(width = 0.5, orientation = "y")) +   geom_point(aes(x = `50%`, shape = error), size = 2,               position = position_dodge(width = 0.5, orientation = "y")              ) +   scale_x_log10("Strain rate (1/s)", breaks = breaks_log(n = 15), labels = label_log()) +   scale_color_manual("Error propagation",     values = c("all" = "#F28E2B", "only flow law" = "#4E79A7", "none" = "#E15759"),     labels = c("all" = "Measurement + flow law parameters", "only flow law" = "Only flow law parameters", "none" = "None")   ) +   labs(y = NULL, caption = paste("95% interpercentile range based on", n, "Monte Carlo simulations")) +   guides(shape = "none") +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Authors and Citation
text: Tobias Stephan. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Stephan T (2026). geocreep: Strain, Stress, Fugacity Calculations Geology. R package version 0.0.0.9000, https://github.com/tobiste/geocreep.
code: @Manual{,   title = {geocreep: Strain, Stress, and Fugacity Calculations for Geology},   author = {Tobias Stephan},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9000},   url = {https://github.com/tobiste/geocreep}, }

what: Strain, Stress, and Fugacity Calculations for Geology
title: Strain, Stress, and Fugacity Calculations for Geology
text: goal geocreep quick easy calculate strain rates, fugacities differential stress grain sizes using R. codes use Monte Carlo simulations propagate uncertainties flow parameters final estimate.

title: Strain, Stress, and Fugacity Calculations for Geology
text: can install development version geocreep GitHub :
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("tobiste/geocreep")

title: Strain, Stress, and Fugacity Calculations for Geology
code: library(geocreep) library(units) # load this package to define your units  # Set seed for reproducibility set.seed(20250411)

title: Strain, Stress, and Fugacity Calculations for Geology
text: Calculate water fugacity temperature pressures using Pitzer Sterner (1994) equation: , Monte Carlo simulation produces 1,000 normal-distributed estimates fugacity.
code: nmc <- 1e3 # create 1,000 samples for temperature and pressure using a normal distribution  # Define temperature and pressure temperature <- units::set_units(rnorm(nmc, 300, 50 / 1.96), degC) pressure <- units::set_units(rnorm(nmc, 400, 100 / 1.96), MPa)  # Calculate fugacity fugacity <- ps_fugacity(pressure, temperature)  fugacity_stats <- summary(fugacity) #> Statistical summary of 1000 Monte Carlo simulations #>  #> Mean:                    380 bar  #> 95% confidence interval: 370 - 390 bar  #> Standard error:          4.12965 #> Student's t-Test:        p<0.05

title: Strain, Stress, and Fugacity Calculations for Geology
text: Calculating differential stress grain size (e.g. using Stipp Tullis, 2003, piezometer): default, functions create 1,000,000 samples parameter uncertainties, creating 1e6 results. Results Monte Carlo simulations based flow law equations follow normal (symmetric) distribution parameters exponents power laws. Therefore, median interpercentile range provide best estimators average dispersion, rather mean standard deviation. Note: also subgrain-size piezometer (Goddard et al. 2021): subgrainsize_piezometer()
code: # Define grain size grainsize <- units::set_units(11, um)  # Calculate equivalent differential stress stress <- grainsize_piezometry(grainsize, model = "Stipp-reg2-3")  # Summary stats of Monte Carlo simulation summary(stress) #> Statistical summary of 1000000 Monte Carlo simulations #>  #> Median:                      100 MPa  #> 95% interpercentile range:   53 - 210 MPa  #> Standard error in log-space: 0.000152958 #> Student's t-Test:            p<0.05

title: Strain, Stress, and Fugacity Calculations for Geology
text: Calculate strain rates using defined flow law dislocation creep quartz differential stress, temperature, fugacity:
code: # Calculate strain rates using temperature, fugacity, and differential stress edot <- creep_quartz(   stress = stress,   temperature = units::set_units(rnorm(1e6, 300, 50 / 1.96), degC),   fugacity = rnorm(1e6, fugacity_stats$mean, fugacity_stats$sd),   model = "Hirth2001" )  # Summary stats of Monte Carlo simulation summary(edot) #> Statistical summary of 1000000 Monte Carlo simulations #>  #> Median:                      1.1e-13 /s #> 95% interpercentile range:   1.8e-18 - 6.8e-09 /s #> Standard error in log-space: 0.00242432 #> Student's t-Test:            p<0.05

title: Strain, Stress, and Fugacity Calculations for Geology
text: Tobias Stephan (tstephan@lakeheadu.ca)

title: Strain, Stress, and Fugacity Calculations for Geology
text: welcome feedback, suggestions, issues, contributions! found bug, please file minimal code reproduce issue.

title: Strain, Stress, and Fugacity Calculations for Geology
text: GPL-3.0 License

what: Strain Rates for Creep in Quartz — creep_quartz
title: Strain Rates for Creep in Quartz — creep_quartz
text: Calculates strain rates deforming quartz stress, temperature, grain size experimentally determined creep law parameters. Monte Carlo simulation used propagating parameter uncertainties creep estimate.

title: Strain Rates for Creep in Quartz — creep_quartz
code: creep_quartz(   stress,   temperature,   fugacity = NULL,   grainsize = NULL,   pressure = NULL,   model = c("Hirth2001", "Paterson1990", "Kronenberg1984", "Luan1992", "Gleason1995",     "Gleason1995_melt", "Rutter2004", "Fukuda2018_LT", "Fukuda2018_HT", "Richter2018",     "Lu2019", "Tokle2019_LT", "Tokle2019_HT", "Lusk2021_LP", "Lusk2021_HP"),   propagate_err = TRUE,   sim = 1e+06 )

title: Strain Rates for Creep in Quartz — creep_quartz
text: stress Differential stress MPa units object temperature Temperature Kelvin units object fugacity Water fugacity MPa units object grainsize Grain size \(\mu\)m units object pressure Pressure MPa units object model character specifying flow law used: "Hirth2001" Hirth Tullis (2001), dislocation creep "Paterson1990" Paterson Luan (1990): dislocation creep; axial compression "Kronenberg1984" Kronenberg Tullis (1984): deformation mechanism: dislocation creep grain-size sensitive creep; strain geometry: axial compression "Luan1992" Luan Paterson (1990): dislocation creep; axial compression "Gleason1995" Gleason Tullis (1995): dislocation creep; axial compression "Gleason1995_melt" Gleason Tullis (1995): dislocation creep; axial compression; 1–2% melt "Rutter2004" Rutter Brodie (2004b): dislocation creep; axial compression "Fukuda2018_LT" Fukada et al. (2018): dislocation creep; axial compression; low temperatures (600–750 °C) "Fukuda2018_HT" Fukada et al. (2018): dislocation creep grain-size sensitive creep; axial compression; high temperatures (800–950 °C) "Richter2018" Richter et al. (2018): dislocation creep grain-size sensitive creep; general shear; 800–1000 °C "Lu2019" Lu Jiang (2019): dislocation creep "Tokle2019_HT" Tokle et al. (2019): dislocation creep grain-size sensitive creep; high temperatures/low stress "Tokle2019_LT" Tokle et al. (2019): dislocation creep grain-size sensitive creep; low temperature/high stress "Lusk2021_LP" Lusk et al. (2021): dislocation-dominated creep wet quartz, low pressures (≤560 MPa) "Lusk2021_HP" Lusk et al. (2021): dislocation-dominated creep wet quartz, high pressures (700–1600 MPa) propagate_err logical. Whether errors flow law parameters propagated. TRUE default. sim non-negative number. Number Monte Carlo simulations

title: Strain Rates for Creep in Quartz — creep_quartz
text: list. Strain rate 1/s. Monte Carlo Simulation used, object class "MCS" returned (see summary() detailed description output). flow laws produce log-normal distributed estimates considering uncertainties parameter. Hence recommended report median (geometric mean), interpercentile range.

title: Strain Rates for Creep in Quartz — creep_quartz
text: General flow law giving strain rate  $$\dot{\varepsilon} = \sigma^n d^m f_{H_2O}^r \, e^{\left({\frac{-H}{RT}}\right)}$$ \(\sigma\) differential stress, \(d\) grain size, \(f_{H_2O}\) water fugacity, \(T\) temperature, \(H\) enthalpy, \(R\) ideal gas constant. flow parameters prefactor \(\), exponents \(n\), \(m\), \(r\). propagate uncertainties flow parameters Monte Carlo simulation used . flow law parameters given mean value marginal error (\(\mu \pm z\)), Monte Carlo simulation assumes normal distribution given \(X = N\left(\mu, \sigma\right)\), \(\mu\) mean \(\sigma\) standard deviation mean (\(\sigma = \text{z}/1.96\)). parameter given range possible values \(\left[x_\text{min}, x_\text{max}\right]\), Monte Carlo simulation assumes uniform distribution given \(X = U\left(x_\text{min}, x_\text{max}\right)\).

title: Strain Rates for Creep in Quartz — creep_quartz
text: Fukuda, J., Holyoke, C. W., & Kronenberg, . K. (2018). Deformation Fine‐Grained Quartz Aggregates Mixed Diffusion Dislocation Creep. (2001). evaluation quartzite flow laws based comparisons experimentally naturally deformed rocks. International Journal Earth Sciences, 90(1), 77-87. doi:10.1007/s005310000152 Kronenberg, . K., & Tullis, J. (1984). Flow strengths quartz aggregates: Grain size pressure effects due hydrolytic weakening. (1992). Preparation Deformation Synthetic Aggregates Quartz. Journal Geophysical Research, 97, 301–320. https://doi.org/10.1029/91JB01748 Lusk, . D. J., Platt, J. P., & Platt, J. . (2021). Natural Experimental Constraints Flow Law Dislocation‐Dominated Creep Wet Quartz. Journal Geophysical Research: Solid Earth, 126(5), 1-25. doi:10.1029/2020JB021302 Paterson, M. S., & Luan, F. C. (1990). Quartzite rheology geological conditions. Geological Society, London, Special Publications, 54(1), 299–307. doi:10.1144/GSL.SP.1990.054.01.26 Richter, B., Stünitz, H., & Heilbronner, R. (2018). brittle--viscous transition polycrystalline quartz: experimental study. Journal Structural Geology, 114(September 2017), 1-21. doi:10.1016/j.jsg.2018.06.005 Rutter, E. H., & Brodie, K. H. (2004a). Experimental grain size-sensitive flow hot-pressed Brazilian quartz aggregates. Journal Structural Geology, 26(11), 2011–2023. doi:10.1016/j.jsg.2004.04.006 Rutter, E. ., & Brodie, K. . (2004b). Experimental intracrystalline plastic flow hot-pressed synthetic quartzite prepared Brazilian quartz crystals. Earth Planetary Science Letters, 505, 152-161. doi:10.1016/j.epsl.2018.10.017

title: Strain Rates for Creep in Quartz — creep_quartz
code: set.seed(20250411) stress <- units::set_units(100, MPa) temperature <- units::set_units(300, degC) pressure <- units::set_units(400, MPa) fugacity <- ps_fugacity(pressure, temperature)  creep_quartz(   stress = stress, temperature = temperature, fugacity = fugacity,   model = "Hirth2001") |>  summary() #> Statistical summary of 1000000 Monte Carlo simulations #>  #> Median:                      1.2e-14 1 / s #> 95% interpercentile range:   4.3e-19 - 3.1e-10 1 / s #> Standard error in log-space: 0.00226041 #> Student's t-Test:            p<0.05

what: Algebraic error propagation of flow laws — creep_quartz_analytic
title: Algebraic error propagation of flow laws — creep_quartz_analytic
text: Uses 1st order Taylor expansion (linearization) natural log-space flow law

text: list. "e_best" Strain rate (1/s) "sd_e_range" Range 1s standard deviation  (1/s) "var_log_e_total" Variance natural log-space "sd_log_e_total Standard deviation natural log-space "var_log_e" Individual variance components natural log-space

title: Algebraic error propagation of flow laws — creep_quartz_analytic
code: stress <- units::set_units(100, MPa) temperature <- units::set_units(300, degC) pressure <- units::set_units(400, MPa) fugacity <- ps_fugacity(pressure, temperature)  creep_quartz_analytic(   stress = stress, temperature = temperature, fugacity = fugacity, pressure = pressure,   model = "Hirth2001") #> $e_best #> 1.165839e-14 [1/s] #>  #> $sd_e_range #> Units: [1/s] #> [1] 1.834746e-16 7.408012e-13 #>  #> $var_log_e_total #> [1] 17.23666 #>  #> $sd_log_e_total #> [1] 4.151706 #>  #> $var_log_e #>          prefactor    stress_exponent  fugacity_exponent grainsize_exponent  #>        1.908683320       15.322485539        0.000000000        0.000000000  #>             stress           fugacity          grainsize        temperature  #>        0.000000000        0.000000000        0.000000000        0.000000000  #>           enthalpy  #>        0.005491873  #>

title: Grain-Size Piezometry — grainsize_piezometry
text: Calculates differential stress grain size. Uses Monte Carlo sampling propagating parameter uncertainties flow model.

title: Grain-Size Piezometry — grainsize_piezometry
code: grainsize_piezometry(   d,   sd = NULL,   model = c("Stipp-reg2-3", "Stripp-reg1", "Cross-1", "Cross-sliding"),   sim = 1e+06,   propagate_err = TRUE )

title: Grain-Size Piezometry — grainsize_piezometry
text: d numeric. Grain size μm units object sd (optional) Standard deviation d model character. One "Stipp-reg2-3" Piezometer dislocation creep regime 2 3 (deviatoric stress <368 MPa) Stipp Tullis (2003) "Stripp-reg1" Piezometer dislocation creep regime 1 (deviatoric stress ≥368 MPa) Stipp Tullis (2003) "Cross-1" Piezometer Cross et al. (2017) 1 μm step size resolution EBSD data "Cross-sliding" Sliding resolution piezometer Cross et al. 2017. According authors, accurately estimates stress fine-grained (<10 μm) samples sim non-negative number. Number Monte Carlo simulations propagate_err logical. Whether errors flow law parameters propagated. TRUE default.

title: Grain-Size Piezometry — grainsize_piezometry
text: list. Differential stress MPa. Monte Carlo Simulation used, object class "MCS" returned (see summary() detailed description output). piezometer produce log-normal distributed estimates considering uncertainties equation parameter. Hence recommended report median (geometric mean), interpercentile range.

title: Grain-Size Piezometry — grainsize_piezometry
text: General formula grain-size piezometer : $$\sigma = \left(\frac{d}{k}\right)^\frac{1}{n}$$ \(\sigma\) differential stress, \(d\) grain size, \(k\) \(n\) empirical parameters.

title: Grain-Size Piezometry — grainsize_piezometry
text: Stipp, M., & Tullis, J. (2003). recrystallized grain size piezometer quartz. Geophysical Research Letters, 30(21), 1–5. https://doi.org/10.1029/2003GL018444 Cross, . J., Prior, D. J., Stipp, M., & Kidder, S. (2017). recrystallized grain size piezometer quartz: EBSD-based calibration. Geophysical Research Letters, 44(13), 6667–6674. https://doi.org/10.1002/2017GL073836

title: Grain-Size Piezometry — grainsize_piezometry
code: set.seed(20250411) grainsize_piezometry(12.2) |> summary() # 92 #> Statistical summary of 1000000 Monte Carlo simulations #>  #> Median:                      92 MPa  #> 95% interpercentile range:   49 - 190 MPa  #> Standard error in log-space: 0.000151644 #> Student's t-Test:            p<0.05 grainsize_piezometry(31) |> summary() # 44 #> Statistical summary of 1000000 Monte Carlo simulations #>  #> Median:                      44 MPa  #> 95% interpercentile range:   24 - 86 MPa  #> Standard error in log-space: 0.00014041 #> Student's t-Test:            p<0.05

title: Peierls Creep — peierls_creep
text: Strain rate low-temperature, plastic deformation mechanism corresponds dislocation glide high stress (differential stress larger 200 MPa).

title: Peierls Creep — peierls_creep
code: peierls_creep(   stress,   temperature,   model = c("Goetze1979", "Demouchy2013", "Mei2010"),   sim = 1e+06 )

title: Peierls Creep — peierls_creep
text: stress Differential stress MPa units object temperature Temperature Kelvin units object model character. Model used, one "Goetze1979" (Goetze Evans, 1979), "Demouchy2013" (Demouchy et al, 2013), "Mei2010" (Mei et al., 2010) sim integer. Number Monte Carlo simulations.

title: Peierls Creep — peierls_creep
text: Strain rate 1/s

title: Peierls Creep — peierls_creep
code: peierls_creep(300, 500, model = "Goetze1979") #> 4.407903e-41 [1/s] peierls_creep(300, 500, model = "Demouchy2013") #> 2.21843e-28 [1/s] peierls_creep(300, 500, model = "Mei2010") |> summary() #>      Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max.  #> 0.000e+00 0.000e+00 0.000e+00 1.037e-23 0.000e+00 7.382e-19

what: Fugacity and molar volume for H\(_2\)O or CO\(_2\) — pitzer
title: Fugacity and molar volume for H\(_2\)O or CO\(_2\) — pitzer
text: calculate  molar volume fugacity H\(_2\)O  CO\(_2\) using Pitzer Sterner (1994) equation state.

title: Fugacity and molar volume for H\(_2\)O or CO\(_2\) — pitzer
code: ps_volume(pressure, temperature, phase = c("H2O", "CO2"))  ps_fugacity(pressure, temperature, phase = c("H2O", "CO2"), ...)

title: Fugacity and molar volume for H\(_2\)O or CO\(_2\) — pitzer
text: modified version fugacity.py script: https://github.com/forsterite/fugacity/tree/master R version uses precise gas constant, accepts inputs different units, adds CO\(_2\) parameters.

title: Fugacity and molar volume for H\(_2\)O or CO\(_2\) — pitzer
text: pressure numeric. Pressure either bar units object temperature numeric. Temperature either Kelvin units object phase character. Fluid phase fugacity volume calculated ; one "H2O" (default) "CO2". ... optional arguments passed future.apply::future_mapply()

title: Fugacity and molar volume for H\(_2\)O or CO\(_2\) — pitzer
text: units object

title: Fugacity and molar volume for H\(_2\)O or CO\(_2\) — pitzer
text: Pitzer, K.S. & Sterner, S.M., 1994. Equations state valid continuously zero extreme pressures H2O CO2. Journal Chemical Physics. 101: 3111-3116.

title: Fugacity and molar volume for H\(_2\)O or CO\(_2\) — pitzer
code: pressure <- units::set_units(1, atm) temperature <- units::set_units(25, degC) ps_volume(pressure, temperature) # 18.7231 #> 18.72311 [cm^3/mol] ps_fugacity(pressure, temperature) # 0.04946 #> 0.04946146 [bar]  temperature2 <- units::set_units(300, degC) pressure2 <- units::set_units(400, MPa) ps_fugacity(pressure2, temperature2) # 37 MPa #> 371.3371 [bar] ps_volume(pressure2, temperature2) #> 18.69728 [cm^3/mol]

what: Subgrain‐Size Piezometer Calibrated for EBSD — subgrainsize_piezometry
title: Subgrain‐Size Piezometer Calibrated for EBSD — subgrainsize_piezometry
text: Calculates differential stress using Subgrain‐Size Piezometer Goddard et al. (2020). Uses Monte Carlo sampling propagating parameter uncertainties flow model.

title: Subgrain‐Size Piezometer Calibrated for EBSD — subgrainsize_piezometry
code: subgrainsize_piezometry(   lambda,   sd = NULL,   calibrated = TRUE,   min = c("q", "fo90", "fo50"),   sim = 1e+06,   propagate_err = TRUE )

title: Subgrain‐Size Piezometer Calibrated for EBSD — subgrainsize_piezometry
text: lambda mean line intercept length μm units object. sd (optional) Standard deviation lambda calibrated logical. Whether calibration Holyoke Kronenberg (2010) considered . min character. Mineral uses. one "q" quartz, "fo90" Olive 90% Forsterite, "fo50" Olivine 50% Forsterite. sim non-negative number. Number Monte Carlo simulations propagate_err logical. Whether errors flow law parameters propagated. TRUE default.

title: Subgrain‐Size Piezometer Calibrated for EBSD — subgrainsize_piezometry
text: list. Differential stress MPa. Monte Carlo Simulation used, object class "MCS" returned (see summary() detailed description output). piezometer produce log-normal distributed estimates considering uncertainties equation parameter. Hence recommended report median (geometric mean), interpercentile range.

title: Subgrain‐Size Piezometer Calibrated for EBSD — subgrainsize_piezometry
text: sub-grain size piezometer $$\frac{\lambda}{b} = 10^\left(\frac{\sigma}{\mu}\right)^b$$ \(\lambda\) mean line intercept length, \(b\) Burgers vector, \(\sigma\) differential stress, \(\mu\) shear modulus, \(\) \(b\) empirical exponents.

title: Subgrain‐Size Piezometer Calibrated for EBSD — subgrainsize_piezometry
text: Goddard, R. M., Hansen, L. N., Wallis, D., Stipp, M., Holyoke, C. W., Kumamoto, K. M., & Kohlstedt, D. L. (2020). Subgrain‐Size Piezometer Calibrated EBSD. Geophysical Research Letters, 47(23). https://doi.org/10.1029/2020GL090056

title: Subgrain‐Size Piezometer Calibrated for EBSD — subgrainsize_piezometry
code: set.seed(20250411) subgrainsize_piezometry(9, min = "fo50") |> summary() # 420 MPa #> Statistical summary of 1000000 Monte Carlo simulations #>  #> Median:                      560 MPa  #> 95% interpercentile range:   34 - 3600 MPa  #> Standard error in log-space: 0.000516207 #> Student's t-Test:            p<0.05 subgrainsize_piezometry(18, min = "q") |> summary() # 240 MPa #> Statistical summary of 1000000 Monte Carlo simulations #>  #> Median:                      220 MPa  #> 95% interpercentile range:   11 - 1500 MPa  #> Standard error in log-space: 0.00054436 #> Student's t-Test:            p<0.05

what: Estimates from Monte Carlo Simulation — summary-MCS
title: Estimates from Monte Carlo Simulation — summary-MCS
text: Estimates Monte Carlo Simulation

title: Estimates from Monte Carlo Simulation — summary-MCS
code: # S3 method for class 'MCS_log' summary(object, unit = NULL, ...)  # S3 method for class 'MCS' summary(object, unit = NULL, ...)

title: Estimates from Monte Carlo Simulation — summary-MCS
text: object numeric vector class "MCS" "MCS_log". values n Monte Carlo Simulations unit (optional) object class units symbolic_units, case set_units expression symbols. ... additional arguments affecting summary produced.

title: Estimates from Monte Carlo Simulation — summary-MCS
text: list. class object "MCS", list contains following elements: median median Monte Carlo simulations ir.95 95% 68% interpercentile range ir.68 68% interpercentile range mean arithmetic mean Monte Carlo simulations sd 1\(\sigma\) standard deviation conf.int 95% confidence intverval mean stderr standard error t.test Statistic p-value Student's t-test n Number samples class object "MCS_log", list contains following elements: median median Monte Carlo simulations ir.95 95% 68% interpercentile range ir.68 68% interpercentile range mean geometric mean Monte Carlo simulations sd 1\(\sigma\) range mean sd2 2\(\sigma\) range mean conf.int 95% confidence intverval mean stderr.log standard error log(samples) t.test Statistic p-value Student's t-test log(samples) n Number samples Values unit specified parameter unit equal unit x x units object.

title: Estimates from Monte Carlo Simulation — summary-MCS
text: Equations form \(X = b^{n \pm \sigma}\) create non-normal, left-skewed distributions (e.g. flow laws, grain-size piezometers). Thus, recommended report median percentiles instead mean, standard deviation confidence intervals.

title: Estimates from Monte Carlo Simulation — summary-MCS
code: set.seed(20250411) MC_res <- grainsize_piezometry(12.2) summary(MC_res) #> Statistical summary of 1000000 Monte Carlo simulations #>  #> Median:                      92 MPa  #> 95% interpercentile range:   49 - 190 MPa  #> Standard error in log-space: 0.000151644 #> Student's t-Test:            p<0.05  n <- 100 temperature <- units::set_units(rnorm(n, 300, 25), degC) pressure <- units::set_units(rnorm(n, 400, 50), MPa) MC_res2 <- ps_fugacity(pressure, temperature) # 37 MPa summary(MC_res2) #> Statistical summary of 100 Monte Carlo simulations #>  #> Mean:                    390 bar  #> 95% confidence interval: 370 - 420 bar  #> Standard error:          11.9467 #> Student's t-Test:        p<0.05
